subsystem: GNOME Screensaver lock window class (gnome-screensaver). This is the GObject class-initialization function gs_window_class_init() for the GSWindow type — the full-screen, override-redirect window that draws the lock/blank screen and is meant to intercept ALL user input (keyboard, pointer, scroll) while the session is locked. The function wires the widget's event-handler vtable, registers two signals (activity/deactivated), and installs the object's GObject properties (obscured, dialog-up, lock/logout/keyboard enablement, logout/keyboard commands, logout timeout, and the target Xinerama monitor).
provenance: Identified from GObject/GTK+ idioms and gnome-screensaver naming: the symbol gs_window_class_init and the GS* prefix (GSWindowClass, GSWindowPrivate, gs_window_finalize, gs_window_real_*) are gnome-screensaver's GSWindow. G_OBJECT_CLASS()/GTK_WIDGET_CLASS() casts, g_signal_new, g_object_class_install_property, and g_param_spec_* are the GLib/GTK type-system API. The sample header comment 'primevul_c_train_2789_vuln' and the file name suffix cwe284.c indicate the label class CWE-284 (Improper Access Control); no CVE id is asserted here because none is provided in the reference notes.
trust_boundary: GSWindow is a security-boundary component: it is the ON-SCREEN lock. The attacker is a person at the physical console (or anything that can inject X input / hotplug a display) attempting to reach the session behind the lock without authenticating. The hostile surface is therefore the WIDGET EVENT VTABLE assigned here (key_press_event, motion_notify_event, button_press_event, scroll_event, grab_broken_event, visibility_notify_event) — these handlers decide whether input reaches the locked session — plus the string command properties (logout-command, keyboard-command) which name programs that will later be spawned, and the Xinerama 'monitor' property that governs whether every display is actually covered. This class_init itself only registers pointers/specs; the security-relevant logic lives in the referenced gs_window_real_* handlers (implementation not provided), so the findings here are latent contracts that those implementations must uphold.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the GObject class-init function for the GSWindow type. GObject calls it exactly once, the first time an instance of the class is created, to populate the class vtable. The single parameter klass is the freshly-allocated GSWindowClass. The body immediately casts it two ways: G_OBJECT_CLASS(klass) yields the base GObjectClass view (for finalize/get_property/set_property), and GTK_WIDGET_CLASS(klass) yields the GtkWidgetClass view (for the widget event/lifecycle vfuncs). Both casts alias the same memory; they are just typed windows onto the class struct. Everything that follows fills fields reachable through these two pointers.

The three core GObject vfuncs are installed. finalize (gs_window_finalize) is the destructor, run when the last reference drops; it must tear down the private data and any grabs/timeouts the window holds. get_property/set_property (gs_window_get_property / gs_window_set_property) are the dispatchers that read and write the named GObject properties installed later in this function — every g_object_set()/g_object_get() on a GSWindow funnels through these, keyed by the PROP_* ids.

Four GtkWidget lifecycle vfuncs are overridden. show/hide (gs_window_real_show / gs_window_real_hide) map/unmap the lock window; for a locker, show is where the window must go full-screen, override-redirect, raised, and grab keyboard+pointer. realize/unrealize (gs_window_real_realize / gs_window_real_unrealize) create and destroy the underlying GdkWindow (X resource). These are the moments where the exclusive input grab that enforces the lock is established and released, so their correctness is part of the access-control boundary.

This block installs the actual user-input handlers for the locked window: key presses, pointer motion, mouse button presses, and scroll. On a lock screen these handlers are the enforcement point — they must fully consume input (or hand it only to the unlock dialog) and must NOT let keystrokes or clicks fall through to the session behind the lock. Any handler that returns the wrong propagation value, or that isn't installed for a class of events, is a direct input-leak / lock-bypass vector. These are the most security-sensitive vfuncs in the table.

get_preferred_width/height are GTK3 size-request vfuncs (benign geometry). The next two are security-critical. grab_broken_event (gs_window_real_grab_broken) fires when the X server revokes the keyboard/pointer grab that the locker relies on for exclusivity — the handler MUST re-establish the grab, or another client can steal focus and reach the session. visibility_notify_event (gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event) fires when the lock window becomes partially/fully obscured — the handler must raise the window back to the top so no other window can cover the locker and expose the session. Together these keep the lock 'on top and exclusive'.

Reserves per-instance private storage of sizeof(GSWindowPrivate) bytes, associated with this class. This is where the window keeps its runtime state (lock/logout/keyboard flags, command strings, timeout, monitor index, grab state, child dialog handle, etc.). It is a standard GObject allocation; it carries no attacker-controlled length.

what_the_code_does.code:
signals [ACTIVITY] =
                g_signal_new ("activity",
                              G_TYPE_FROM_CLASS (object_class),
                              G_SIGNAL_RUN_LAST,
                              G_STRUCT_OFFSET (GSWindowClass, activity),
                              NULL,
                              NULL,
                              gs_marshal_BOOLEAN__VOID,
                              G_TYPE_BOOLEAN,
                              0);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Registers the 'activity' signal, stored in signals[ACTIVITY]. It uses the class handler slot GSWindowClass.activity (via G_STRUCT_OFFSET), takes no arguments, and returns G_TYPE_BOOLEAN through the gs_marshal_BOOLEAN__VOID marshaller. Semantically the locker emits 'activity' when it detects the user is present (input arrived) so the parent screensaver logic can decide to raise the unlock dialog; the boolean return lets a handler veto/consume the activity. It is control-flow plumbing, not a data buffer.

what_the_code_does.code:
signals [DEACTIVATED] =
                g_signal_new ("deactivated",
                              G_TYPE_FROM_CLASS (object_class),
                              G_SIGNAL_RUN_LAST,
                              G_STRUCT_OFFSET (GSWindowClass, deactivated),
                              NULL,
                              NULL,
                              g_cclosure_marshal_VOID__VOID,
                              G_TYPE_NONE,
                              0);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Registers the 'deactivated' signal (signals[DEACTIVATED]) using the GSWindowClass.deactivated class slot, no arguments, no return (G_TYPE_NONE via g_cclosure_marshal_VOID__VOID). This is emitted when the window is dismissed / the session is being unlocked, letting the manager unmap and destroy the locker. Note that a handler connected to a signal can run application code synchronously during emission — a general GObject re-entrancy consideration — but this registration itself is standard.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_OBSCURED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("obscured",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READABLE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_DIALOG_UP,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("dialog-up",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READABLE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Installs two READ-ONLY boolean properties. 'obscured' (PROP_OBSCURED) exposes whether the lock window is currently covered by something else — it reflects the visibility-notify state and defaults FALSE. 'dialog-up' (PROP_DIALOG_UP) exposes whether the unlock/authentication dialog is currently displayed, default FALSE. Both are G_PARAM_READABLE only, so external code can query but not set them; they are status flags, not attack inputs, but 'obscured' is meaningful because a locker that is obscured is not enforcing the lock.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOCK_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("lock-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOGOUT_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("logout-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Installs two writable boolean policy flags, both defaulting FALSE. 'lock-enabled' (PROP_LOCK_ENABLED) controls whether the window actually requires authentication to dismiss versus being a mere blank/idle screen — the single most security-defining switch on the object. 'logout-enabled' (PROP_LOGOUT_ENABLED) controls whether a 'Log Out' affordance is offered from the lock screen. Both are G_PARAM_READWRITE, so any code holding the GSWindow (the manager) can toggle them; the security question is whether an attacker-reachable path can flip lock-enabled off.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOGOUT_TIMEOUT,
                                         g_param_spec_long ("logout-timeout",
                                                            NULL,
                                                            NULL,
                                                            -1,
                                                            G_MAXLONG,
                                                            0,
                                                            G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Installs 'logout-timeout' (PROP_LOGOUT_TIMEOUT) as a glong with range [-1, G_MAXLONG] and default 0. It is the number of seconds the window must be locked before the logout button appears. The spec permits -1 (sentinel for 'disabled') up to G_MAXLONG. It is writable (G_PARAM_READWRITE). A signed long spanning to G_MAXLONG feeding a timer/arithmetic in the implementation is worth noting for overflow hygiene, but on its own this is a policy value, not a buffer.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOGOUT_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("logout-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Installs 'logout-command' (PROP_LOGOUT_COMMAND) as a writable string (default NULL). This is the command line the locker will SPAWN when the user activates 'Log Out' from the lock screen. A string property that names a program to execute is a privileged sink: whoever can set it, and however it is later parsed/executed, determines what runs in the locked user's context. This is the property to trace to its exec site.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("keyboard-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("keyboard-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Installs the on-screen-keyboard pair. 'keyboard-enabled' (PROP_KEYBOARD_ENABLED) boolean, default FALSE, controls whether an embedded on-screen keyboard is shown on the lock screen (an accessibility feature). 'keyboard-command' (PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND) writable string, default NULL, is the command line for the OSK helper program that the locker spawns and embeds. Like logout-command this is an EXEC sink; additionally, running an external program (and embedding its window) inside the lock screen widens the trusted surface of the locker itself.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_MONITOR,
                                         g_param_spec_int ("monitor",
                                                           "Xinerama monitor",
                                                           "The monitor (in terms of Xinerama) which the window is on",
                                                           0,
                                                           G_MAXINT,
                                                           0,
                                                           G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT));

}
what_the_code_does.explanation: Installs 'monitor' (PROP_MONITOR) as an int in [0, G_MAXINT], default 0, marked G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT (settable at construction). The nickname 'Xinerama monitor' and blurb make explicit that one GSWindow instance covers ONE physical monitor. This is architecturally important: the locker enforces coverage by creating one such window per monitor. The correctness of full-screen coverage — and what happens when the monitor set changes at runtime (hotplug / Xinerama reconfiguration) — is an access-control property, because any uncovered monitor exposes the session. The trailing brace closes gs_window_class_init.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This vtable is the enforcement point of a screen lock, and it is a LATENT access-control surface: the contract is that while locked, the referenced handlers must consume every input event and keep the window exclusively grabbed and on top. Two of them are the classic gnome-screensaver bypass surfaces. grab_broken_event (gs_window_real_grab_broken) fires when the X server takes the keyboard/pointer grab away from the locker (another client can request a grab, or a VT switch/RANDR event can break it); if the handler does not immediately re-grab, an attacker at the console regains keyboard/mouse control of the unlocked session behind the lock. visibility_notify_event (gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event) fires when the lock window is obscured; if the handler does not raise the window back to the top, another window can be stacked over the locker, exposing the session. Likewise if key_press_event/button_press_event/scroll_event ever return FALSE (propagate) for events they should swallow, input leaks to underlying widgets. The attacker controls timing (when to request a grab, when to map a competing window, when to hotplug/VT-switch); impact is a full authentication bypass of the lock. This class_init only assigns the pointers, so the bug, if present, lives in the gs_window_real_* implementations (not provided).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_MONITOR,
                                         g_param_spec_int ("monitor",
                                                           "Xinerama monitor",
                                                           "The monitor (in terms of Xinerama) which the window is on",
                                                           0,
                                                           G_MAXINT,
                                                           0,
                                                           G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The 'monitor' property encodes the assumption 'one lock window per Xinerama monitor'. The failure mode is a runtime display change: if a monitor is HOT-PLUGGED (or Xinerama/RANDR is reconfigured, or resolution grows) while the screen is locked, and the manager does not create a covering GSWindow for the new/enlarged region, that region shows the live session — the attacker plugs in (or enables) a display and reads/interacts with the unlocked desktop next to the lock. This is a documented category of screen-locker bypass. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: on every monitors-changed / configure event, ensure every pixel of every monitor is covered by a locked, grabbed GSWindow before input is accepted. It is latent here (the coverage logic is in the manager/implementation), but the property being per-monitor with G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT is direct evidence of the design that must be defended.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
g_param_spec_string ("logout-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));

        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("keyboard-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("keyboard-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: logout-command and keyboard-command are writable string properties that name programs the locker will SPAWN from the lock screen (the logout action and the on-screen-keyboard helper). The latent risk is command execution: if these strings are built from, or overridable by, less-trusted input (e.g. a GSettings key writable by the user, or concatenated arguments), and the implementation passes them through a shell (g_spawn_command_line_*/system) rather than an argv exec, an attacker can inject additional commands that run in the locked user's session. Even without shell injection, embedding an external keyboard process inside the lock screen enlarges the locker's trusted surface (a crashing or misbehaving OSK must not tear down or bypass the grab). The invariant: command strings must come from a trusted config source and be executed via argv (no shell), and helper processes must never be able to disturb the input grab. Latent — the spawn site is not in this file.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOCK_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("lock-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: 'lock-enabled' defaults FALSE and is writable. It is the master switch that decides whether dismissing the window requires authentication. The failure mode is a policy/state bug: if the manager ever creates or leaves a GSWindow with lock-enabled FALSE at a moment the session should be locked (a race during lock setup, an error path, or a settings-change handler that resets it), the window can be dismissed with no authentication — a silent lock bypass. Because the default is FALSE, the safe behavior depends entirely on the implementation explicitly setting it TRUE and never clearing it while locked. Latent; verify the state machine that toggles this property.

summary: This is gnome-screensaver's GSWindow class_init — GObject boilerplate that registers the lock window's event-handler vtable, two signals, and its properties. There is NO memory-safety bug in this registration code itself; its security weight is as the ATTACK-SURFACE map of a screen locker (CWE-284). The load-bearing contracts are latent in the implementations it references: the input/grab/visibility handlers must keep the lock exclusive and on top (grab_broken and visibility_notify are the classic bypass points), the per-monitor 'monitor' property must guarantee full coverage across display hotplug, and the logout/keyboard command strings are exec sinks that must come from trusted config.

reviewed_anchors.reason: GObject class-init entry; runs once at type registration with a trusted class pointer, no attacker input.
Destructor pointer; lifecycle cleanup, not attacker-reachable data. Correctness relevant only for grab/timeout teardown in impl.
Standard property dispatchers; carry no fixed buffer here — the risk lives in the individual property sinks enumerated separately.
Map/unmap vfuncs; grab establishment happens here but is covered by the grab/visibility finding rather than duplicated.
GdkWindow create/destroy; X-resource lifecycle, not a length/buffer contract.
Locked-screen input handlers must consume input and not propagate to the session — core access-control surface (CWE-284).
Geometry size-request vfuncs; no security contract.
Must re-establish the keyboard/pointer grab when broken, or the lock is bypassed; folded into the vtable access-control finding.
Must raise the locker when obscured, else another window covers it and exposes the session; folded into the vtable access-control finding.
Fixed per-instance allocation sized by sizeof; no attacker-controlled length.
Signal registration; control-flow plumbing with no data buffer or untrusted length.
Signal registration; generic GObject emission re-entrancy noted but not a concrete bug here.
Read-only status flag reflecting obscured state; not settable, so not an input, though it signals a lock-coverage condition.
Read-only status flag; not attacker-writable.
Master switch for whether auth is required; a wrong state = silent lock bypass (CWE-284).
Enables a logout affordance; policy toggle whose exec risk is captured under logout-command.
Signed timeout value; overflow hygiene worth noting but it is a policy scalar, not a buffer, and range is intentional (-1 sentinel).
String naming a program to spawn from the lock screen — exec sink; shell-injection/untrusted-source risk (CWE-284/CWE-77).
Enables the on-screen keyboard; the associated risk is in keyboard-command's spawn/embed.
String naming the OSK helper spawned and embedded inside the locker — exec sink and trusted-surface expansion; folded with logout-command.
Per-monitor coverage assumption; display hotplug/Xinerama reconfig can leave a monitor uncovered = lock bypass (CWE-284).

findings.title: Lock-screen input/grab/visibility handler vtable must keep the lock exclusive (latent bypass)
findings.anchor: widget_class->key_press_event / button_press_event / motion_notify_event / scroll_event / grab_broken_event / visibility_notify_event
findings.bug_class: Improper access control / screen-lock bypass (input propagation and grab loss)
findings.invariant: While locked, every installed input handler must consume its event without propagating to underlying widgets/session; gs_window_real_grab_broken must immediately re-acquire the keyboard+pointer grab; gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event must raise the window back to the top whenever it is obscured — the locker must remain exclusively grabbed and topmost at all times.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker at the console requests an X grab (or triggers a VT switch / RANDR event) that breaks the locker's grab; if gs_window_real_grab_broken fails to re-grab, keyboard/mouse reach the unlocked session behind the lock. Alternatively, a competing window is stacked over the locker; if gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event does not re-raise it, the session is exposed. If any key/button/scroll handler returns FALSE (propagate) for events it should swallow, input leaks through. Impact: full authentication bypass of the screen lock.
findings.what_to_confirm: In gs_window_real_grab_broken confirm the grab is re-established (gdk_keyboard/pointer grab or gtk grab) and the event is handled; in gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event confirm the window is raised on obscure; confirm key_press/button_press/motion/scroll handlers return TRUE (stop propagation) for all events not routed to the unlock dialog.

findings.title: Per-monitor 'monitor' property assumes full coverage; display hotplug can leave a monitor uncovered
findings.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_MONITOR,
                                         g_param_spec_int ("monitor",
                                                           "Xinerama monitor",
                                                           "The monitor (in terms of Xinerama) which the window is on",
                                                           0,
                                                           G_MAXINT,
                                                           0,
                                                           G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT));
findings.bug_class: Improper access control / incomplete screen coverage on display reconfiguration
findings.invariant: There must be exactly one locked, grabbed GSWindow covering every monitor at all times; on every monitors-changed / RANDR / Xinerama-reconfigure / resolution-change event the manager must create/resize covering windows for any new or enlarged region BEFORE input is accepted, so no pixel of any monitor ever shows the live session.
findings.failure_mode: With the screen locked, the attacker hot-plugs a display or enables/reconfigures Xinerama so a new monitor (or an enlarged desktop region) appears. If the manager does not spawn a covering GSWindow for it, the uncovered region renders the unlocked session, which the attacker can view and interact with — a lock bypass without authentication.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the GSWindow manager subscribes to monitors-changed/size-changed signals and creates or resizes a covering, grabbed lock window for every monitor before allowing dismissal; confirm no window-count/index off-by-one leaves the last monitor uncovered.

findings.title: logout-command / keyboard-command are exec sinks spawned from the lock screen
findings.code:
g_param_spec_string ("logout-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));

        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("keyboard-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("keyboard-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
findings.bug_class: Command execution from untrusted configuration (exec sink)
findings.invariant: The command strings must originate only from a trusted config source and must be executed via an argv-style exec (no shell), and any spawned helper (on-screen keyboard) must be sandboxed so it cannot disturb the input grab or the lock window's exclusivity.
findings.failure_mode: If the logout-command / keyboard-command strings are derived from a user-writable setting and later passed through a shell (e.g. g_spawn_command_line_sync/system), an attacker who can influence the setting injects extra commands that run in the locked user's session context. Independently, embedding an external keyboard process inside the locker widens the trusted surface: a crashing or hostile OSK process must not be able to break the grab or take over the lock window.
findings.what_to_confirm: Locate the spawn site for logout-command and keyboard-command; confirm argv exec (g_spawn with parsed argv, not command_line/shell) and that the source of these strings is a trusted GSettings/schema, not attacker-writable; confirm the embedded OSK cannot break the grab.

findings.title: lock-enabled defaults FALSE; a wrong state yields a no-auth dismissal
findings.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOCK_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("lock-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
findings.bug_class: Improper access control / lock state machine
findings.invariant: Whenever the session is meant to be locked, the GSWindow must have lock-enabled TRUE, and no code path (settings-change handler, error path, or setup race) may clear it while the window is up; a window that is dismissable without authentication must never be presented as a lock.
findings.failure_mode: Because the property defaults FALSE, safety depends on the implementation explicitly setting it TRUE. If a race during lock setup, an error path, or a settings handler leaves lock-enabled FALSE while the window is shown, the user (or attacker) can dismiss the window with no authentication, bypassing the lock.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace where lock-enabled is set and read; confirm it is TRUE before the window is mapped for a real lock and is never reset to FALSE while locked; confirm the dismissal/unlock path checks it.

audit_checklist: gs_window_real_grab_broken: verify it re-acquires the keyboard AND pointer grab immediately (and retries) so a broken grab cannot leave the session reachable.
gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event: verify it raises the lock window to the top on any obscure event so no window can cover the locker.

gs_window_real_key_press_event / button_press_event / motion_notify_event / scroll_event: verify each returns TRUE (stops propagation) for events not explicitly routed to the unlock dialog, so input never reaches the session.
Monitor coverage: verify the manager reacts to monitors-changed/RANDR/Xinerama events and covers every (new or resized) monitor with a grabbed lock window before accepting input.
logout-command and keyboard-command: verify they are sourced from trusted config and executed via argv (no shell), and that the embedded on-screen keyboard cannot disturb the grab.
lock-enabled state machine: verify it is TRUE for a genuine lock and is never cleared while the window is displayed; confirm the default FALSE is always overridden before mapping.
logout-timeout (glong to G_MAXLONG): verify timer arithmetic on this value cannot overflow and that -1 is handled as the intended 'disabled' sentinel.
gs_window_finalize: verify it releases any active grabs and cancels timeouts so teardown cannot leave a dangling grab or fire a callback into freed state.
